frameworks.name: cortical layer compartmentalization of feedforward and feedback
frameworks.one_line: feedforward and feedback signals terminate in distinct V1 layers, segregating external from internal contents

frameworks.name: mental imagery and mental rotation in early visual cortex
frameworks.one_line: internally-generated visual contents recruit V1 alongside perceived input

frameworks.name: laminar fMRI of early visual cortex
frameworks.one_line: high-resolution depth-resolved fMRI as a tool to dissociate laminar signals

core_question: How does V1 keep perceived and internally-generated (mentally rotated) visual contents from mixing?
key_assumptions: feedforward inputs terminate in middle cortical layers of V1
feedback inputs terminate in superficial and deep layers of V1
perceived and mentally rotated contents both produce decodable V1 representations
manipulations: task condition: veridical perception vs mental rotation
readout across superficial, middle, and deep cortical depth bins
key_findings: mentally rotated contents are stronger at superficial and deep cortical depths
perceived contents are stronger at the middle cortical depth
perception and internally-generated contents are dissociable across V1 layers
interpretations: laminar compartmentalization in V1 functionally segregates external from internal visual contents
feedforward/feedback laminar division supports multiple cognitive functions within a single area
